text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic cholecystectomy with classical port placements: epigastric, hypogastric, lumbar, and umbilical camera port. The gallbladder is retracted upward using the lumbar port, revealing an abnormal bulge in the gallbladder area. Dissection of the peritoneum over the swelling exposes a cord-like structure running downward.

The surgeon removes the peritoneum above the gallbladder, revealing an outpouching identified as Hartmann's pouch. The cystic artery appears taut and runs abnormally towards the right and anterior to the cystic duct. The dissection of Hartmann's pouch is completed, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect the cystic artery. A long cystic duct is visible, along with the junction between the cystic duct and common bile duct (CBD), clearly showing the T junction and reducing the risk of CBD injury.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, which is abnormally positioned towards the right side of the cystic duct. A window is created between the cystic artery and the cystic duct, ensuring the artery leads into the body of the gallbladder. The surgeon carefully clips the cystic artery using LT clips, placing two clips on each side.

The surgeon divides the cystic artery, enhancing the mobility of the gallbladder and clarifying the anatomy. The abnormal outpouching of Hartman's pouch is now part of the gallbladder. A classical posterior window is created, followed by the dissection of the cystic duct, with unnamed vessels around it being divided. The infundibulum cystic duct junction is identified, and the anatomy is clearly visible.

The surgeon creates a wide posterior window and confirms the critical view of safety before ligating and dividing the cystic duct. An additional vessel is observed running parallel to the cystic duct, which is skeletonized completely. A Tenomum clip is applied above the cystic duct CBD junction, ensuring ample length of the cystic duct is visible. Clips are placed on the proximal side, and the surgeon prepares to clip the distal side at the cystic duct infundibulum junction.

The surgeon divides the cystic duct between pairs of clips, then proceeds to detach the gallbladder from its bed by dividing the mesogallbladder or peritoneal fold using a harmonic scalpel. The peritoneal attachment to the liver is slowly divided, ensuring gentle dissection similar to the approach used in the Calot's triangle. The gallbladder is removed in a reverse manner from the fundus, completing the dissection.

The surgeon completes the dissection, detaching the gallbladder from the liver bed. The gallbladder is then extracted through the epigastric tenenum port using a clofosub's instrument, which grips the cystic duct end for removal.
